title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation -- Follow-on testing for detection of drug resistance after TB confirmation
content: Tuberculous pleural effusions are challenging to diagnose. Adenosine deaminase (ADA) detection in pleural fluid supports the diagnosis with a pooled sensitivity and specificity of approximately 92% and 90%, respectively. ADA detection is simple and inexpensive to perform and provides a rapid result. Detection of ADA in cerebral spinal fluid for the diagnosis of TB meningitis has a pooled sensitivity and specificity of approximately 85% and 90%. Results from a few studies have suggested the value of ADA levels of peritoneal and pericardial fluid for suspected TB at these sites. [52] [53] [54]

title: Thoracentesis -- Indications -- Diagnostic Indications for Thoracentesis
content: When thoracentesis is indicated for diagnosis, the Light criteria are instrumental in distinguishing between exudates and transudates. The analysis of pleural aspirate should routinely include a Gram stain, differential cell count, culture, cytology, protein levels, l-lactate dehydrogenase, and pH. Additional tests, such as tuberculosis (TB) screening, should be considered based on individual patient risk factors and regional prevalence. Typically, diagnostic thoracentesis extracts a small volume (20-30 mL) of accumulated fluid. Diagnostic indications for thoracentesis include:

title: Pleurisy -- Evaluation
content: In cases of pleural effusion, thoracentesis may be necessary to analyze pleural fluid and identify the underlying cause. Ultrasound of the chest can guide the procedure by locating the fluid and possibly detecting pleural masses, while computed tomography (CT) may be required for further clarification of the cause. Fluid obtained during thoracentesis is tested for pH, glucose, cell count, lactate dehydrogenase, and bacterial Gram stain and cultures. Clinicians use Light criteria to classify the effusion as either a transudate or exudate. Specific tests, such as an acid-fast bacillus smear, adenosine deaminase level, and tuberculosis culture, are required for patients with suspected tuberculosis.

title: Genitourinary Tuberculosis -- Evaluation -- Selecting Specific Diagnostic Testing for Genitourinary Tuberculosis
content: The selection of diagnostic methods depends on their availability, cost, the degree of clinical suspicion, renal function, and the patient's clinical presentation. Contact your local hospital laboratory or reference lab to determine which tests are available. At least 1 or more Mycobacterium tuberculosis identification tests are typically used, with a positive culture being considered the gold standard. Adjunctive testing is used as needed.

title: Tuberculosis Screening -- Indications -- Test Selection
content: Simultaneous or sequential testing is not recommended, except in cases where the initial test is negative, indeterminate, or borderline if the clinical suspicion is high. In that case, repeat testing with TST or IGRA (depending on which test was done initially) can be done. Additionally, the TST or IGRA should not be repeated if they were previously positive or to follow therapy, as this positive result is expected to be retained, and differences in results are challenging to interpret. [13]

title: Tuberculosis Overview -- Evaluation
content: The sensitivity and specificity of diagnostic tests can vary significantly depending on factors that include pretest probability, immunological status and age of the patient, timing of the test, adherence to proper test procedures, adequacy of specimen collection, and the ability to interpret test results in the context of the patient's risk factors and immune function. See Table. Tuberculosis Tests and Their Advantages and Disadvantages. Radiological images may demonstrate normal to markedly abnormal, nonspecific findings. Without a confirmatory culture or molecular assay, TB is presumptive and based on the degree of clinical suspicion concurrent with surrogate markers of infection. Both under and over-diagnosis occur, depending on the epidemiological setting. [17] [32] [33] [34] [35] [36]

title: Pleural Friction Rub -- Evaluation -- Infection
content: Patients with poor immune status, including people with human immunodeficiency virus and CD4 counts below 100 cells/mm³, should also undergo blood and urine mycobacterial cultures. Clinicians obtain a pleural fluid specimen for AFB smears, mycobacterial cultures, and measurement of adenosine deaminase (ADA), an enzyme produced from lymphocytes and involved in purine metabolism. [16] AFB smears and mycobacterial cultures of pleural fluid are only positive in approximately 50% of cases, and the results can take several weeks. Pleural fluid ADA levels can establish a presumptive diagnosis in patients with lymphocyte-predominant effusions and who live in areas with a high tuberculosis incidence. The pleural fluid in patients with tuberculosis almost always has a total protein content above 4 g/dL. [17]

title: Ascites -- Evaluation
content: Additional tests may be performed only if a specific diagnosis is suspected clinically. LDH and glucose level should be determined in suspected cases of secondary peritonitis. Other tests to consider include amylase (greater than 1000 U/L suggests pancreatic ascites). Mycobacterial culture should be performed only if tuberculosis is strongly suspected. Other ascitic fluid indices such as lactate and pH offer little to no additional information. Chest x-ray may reveal elevated diaphragm.

title: Tuberculous Meningitis -- Evaluation
content: Various new modalities for testing for antigens and antibodies specific to TBM exist using polymerase chain reaction, but they have not won wide acceptance or utilization due to a lack of access to the testing and high variability in the specificity of the test results. The choice of diagnosis in most cases will depend on the resources available. Despite advances in developing improved and accurate diagnostic modalities, MTB confirmation by culture in the CSF remains the gold standard. [2] [3] [9] Culture allows for the assessment of drug sensitivity results. Drug-resistant MTB carries up to twice the mortality. [9] Other tests that can be utilized are antigen testing in the urine and adenosine deaminase. [10] Recent study results have estimated the sensitivity of several diagnostic tests as follows: Ziehl-Neelsen smear (large volume CSF that is centrifuged to concentrate MTB): 34% GeneXpert: 25% MTB culture: 32% [11] CSF lipoarabinomannan: 22% [12] GeneXpert Ultra: 44% [12]

title: Pleurisy -- Evaluation
content: Patients with intermediate risk, with a Wells score of 2 to 6, should undergo a serum D-dimer evaluation. If the D-dimer is less than 500 ng/mL, pulmonary embolism can be effectively excluded without further testing. However, some experts recommend imaging for patients with a Wells score of 4 to 6 due to their higher risk of pulmonary embolism. Serum D-dimer testing is unnecessary for high-risk patients with a Wells score greater than 6, as the D-dimer result is less reliable for excluding a pulmonary embolism in these cases. These patients should proceed directly to CPTA (see Table 2 ).

title: Malignant Pleural Effusion -- Evaluation -- Diagnostic thoracentesis
content: The standard panel of tests that must be performed on a pleural fluid sample includes pleural fluid protein, glucose, pH, lactate dehydrogenase, cytology, and microbiology. [111] About 40 to 60 cc of pleural fluid is considered optimum for diagnosing MPE. [112] While the yield of pleural fluid analysis approaches 6% to 32% for diagnosing mesothelioma, the test has been shown to have a comparatively higher sensitivity in diagnosing adenocarcinoma (80%). [113] [114] While primarily exudative, transudative MPE may be seen in 5% to 10% of cases. [115] Repeat procedures may increase the yield by a third; however, more than 2 repeat procedures are less productive. [116]

title: Pleural Effusion -- Evaluation
content: Condition-specific markers, such as acid-fast bacilli smear, Mycobacterium tuberculosis culture, and adenosine deaminase, are indicated when tuberculosis is suspected. Amylase levels are evaluated if pancreatitis-related effusion is apparent. Pleural fluid triglycerides greater than 110 mg/dL signify a chylothorax. The pleural fluid in such conditions typically has a milky-white appearance. Hemothorax can be established if the pleural fluid hematocrit is more than 0.5 times that of the serum hematocrit.

title: Miliary Tuberculosis -- Treatment / Management
content: Although substantial evidence is lacking, corticosteroid use in miliary TB has shown clinical efficacy in a few scenarios. Notably, it has been demonstrated to be beneficial in adrenal insufficiency, TB meningitis, large pericardial or pleural effusion, IRIS, ARDS, immune-complex nephritis, and secondary hemophagocytic syndrome. [64] [65] Liver function tests (LFTs) should be obtained before and during anti-TB therapy (ATT). Serial LFTs should be performed to check for ATT-induced hepatitis. The criteria for ATT-induced hepatitis have evolved and include the following: Transaminases are elevated up to 5 times the upper normal limit without hepatitis symptoms; or Transaminases are elevated up to 3 times the upper normal limit with hepatitis symptoms; or Bilirubin rises twice the upper normal limit, and other possible hepatitis causes, like acute viral hepatitis and autoimmune hepatitis, have been ruled out.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1286
content: The subdivision of thorax, identified in Arabic as دبيلة, corresponds to the pleural membrane and is directly linked to pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. These three conditions all involve the pleura: pleurisy denotes inflammation of the pleural layers, pneumothorax refers to the presence of air within the pleural space that disrupts normal lung expansion, and pulmonary collapse (atelectasis) often results from pleural pathology that prevents the lung from remaining inflated. Recognizing that the subdivision of thorax (دبيلة) is the anatomical structure underlying these disorders is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate therapeutic intervention, and effective patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 3 -- Community 1623
content: Closed drainage of chest is classified as a subdivision of thorax, representing a specific thoracic intervention used to treat conditions such as pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. By placing a closed drainage system within the thoracic cavity, clinicians can evacuate air, fluid, or blood, thereby re‑expanding the lung and relieving the pathological processes associated with pleurisy, pneumothorax, and pulmonary collapse. This procedure exemplifies how targeted thoracic techniques are applied to manage distinct intrathoracic disorders, highlighting the interrelationship between procedural categories (subdivision of thorax) and the diseases they address.

content: The mediastinum is a central thoracic subdivision that serves as the anatomic site for a spectrum of lower‑respiratory conditions classified under “other lower respiratory disease” (اضطراب منصفي), including mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية), lung‑mediastinal abscesses, and mediastinal effusion. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary imaging modality for evaluating mediastinal pathology. Chest CT—performed as a diagnostic thoracic CT protocol that may begin without contrast and continue with contrast, or as CT angiography of the thorax with contrast—provides the “has_finding_method” link to a variety of mediastinal findings: mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, a general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, and mediastinal effusion. Chest CT also identifies mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, recorded with the “has_direct_procedure_site” relationship to the mediastinum, and specifies the laterality of the fistula (bilateral, right‑sided, left‑sided). Therapeutically, closed drainage of the chest is associated with the thoracic subdivision and may be employed to manage complications such as mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. Together, the anatomical context of the mediastinum, its disease categorization, the detailed CT imaging techniques, the specific radiologic findings, and the related interventional procedures constitute an integrated framework for diagnosing and treating mediastinal and thoracic disorders.

title: UMLS -- Run 24 -- Level 3 -- Community 2425
content: Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and Computerized axial tomography of thorax with contrast are both specific forms of a ct scan chest. The non‑contrast diagnostic CT of the thorax represents a narrower variant of the general ct scan chest procedure, while the contrast‑enhanced axial tomography is classified as a type of ct scan chest. Chest CT imaging is employed to evaluate intrathoracic pathology, and it can reveal findings such as a mass of the mid mediastinum (computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum) and a miliary pulmonary infiltrate (computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate). These relationships illustrate how different CT techniques are categorized under the broader ct scan chest modality and how they are used to detect distinct thoracic abnormalities.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1686
content: Closed drainage of chest is linked to a specific Subdivision of thorax, indicating that this procedure is performed within a defined thoracic region. The Subdivision of thorax is encompassed by the mediastinum, the central compartment of the thoracic cavity that contains vital structures. Imaging of the mediastinum with thoracic CT scans can directly identify mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, which may be present bilaterally, on the right side, or on the left side. Recognizing these fistulas on thoracic CT scans of the mediastinum is essential for guiding the appropriate use of closed chest drainage and for planning further therapeutic interventions.

title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2419
content: The diagnostic protocol termed “Computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material, followed by contrast material(s) and further sections” represents a more specific form of a chest CT scan, indicating that a non‑contrast thoracic CT is performed first and then followed by contrast‑enhanced imaging with additional sections. This refined chest CT technique is employed to evaluate thoracic pathology and serves as a method for detecting distinct radiographic findings. In particular, chest CT imaging can reveal a mass located in the mid mediastinum, as described by “computed tomography of chest: mass of mid mediastinum,” and can also identify a pattern of miliary pulmonary infiltrates, referred to as “computed tomography of chest: miliary pulmonary infiltrate.” Recognizing these specific findings on a detailed chest CT study aids clinicians in diagnosing mediastinal lesions and diffuse pulmonary disease, thereby informing appropriate patient management.

title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 4 -- Community 2868
content: Abnormal chest X‑ray findings (الأشعة السينية على الصَدْر غير طبيعية) are clinically associated with alcohol consumption (تعاطي الكحول). In patients who consume alcohol, clinicians should consider that pulmonary imaging may reveal atypical or pathological changes, which can stem from alcohol‑related lung injury, aspiration events, infections, or other respiratory complications. Recognizing this association prompts healthcare providers to inquire about alcohol use when interpreting abnormal chest radiographs, to assess for potential alcohol‑related pulmonary disease, and to incorporate appropriate counseling or treatment for alcohol misuse into the overall patient care plan.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1217
content: Abces van long en mediastinum, a form of خراج (abscess formation) that belongs to the category of other lower respiratory disease, is linked to اضطراب منصفي (segmental disorder) and frequently manifests as كتلة منصفية (mediastinal mass) within the mediastinum. The mediastinum, situated in the Subdivision of thorax, serves as a common anatomical site for direct procedural findings on thoracic CT imaging, including mediastinal‑pleural fistulas that may be unilateral (right or left), bilateral, or localized. Computed tomography of the chest (ct scan chest) is the primary diagnostic modality for detecting a spectrum of mediastinal and pulmonary abnormalities such as mass of the mid mediastinum, mass of the posterior mediastinum, mass of the superior mediastinum, general mediastinal mass, mediastinal mass containing calcium, mediastinal effusion, and miliary pulmonary infiltrate. Mediastinal effusion is classified as a type of اضطراب منصفي. Various CT techniques are employed, ranging from CT angiography of thorax with contrast (a specific ct scan chest isa) to diagnostic thoracic CT without contrast, highlighting the breadth of imaging options. Findings are reported through computed tomography, thorax, diagnostic; without contrast material and related ct scan chest subtypes. Therapeutically, closed drainage of chest is performed within the thoracic anatomical framework to manage these pathologies. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how lower respiratory diseases, particularly those involving abscesses, can involve the mediastinum; how mediastinal pathology—including masses, effusions, calcifications, diffuse infiltrates, and mediastinal‑pleural communications—is visualized and classified through diverse CT modalities; and how procedural interventions such as closed chest drainage are integrated into patient care.

content: The mediastinum is a central subdivision of the thorax, representing a broader anatomical region within the chest cavity. Thoracic computed tomography (CT) scans are performed directly at the mediastinum to assess mediastinal‑pleural fistulas. These imaging procedures can identify fistulous connections that are present bilaterally, as well as those confined to the right side or the left side of the mediastinum. By targeting the mediastinum, thoracic CT scans provide detailed visualization of mediastinal‑pleural fistulas, facilitating accurate diagnosis and guiding appropriate clinical management.

content: The community describes a network of thoracic and mediastinal entities, disorders, imaging modalities, and interventional procedures that together define the assessment and management of lower respiratory disease. Lung and mediastinal abscess (خراج) is classified as an “other lower respiratory disease” and is linked to mediastinal pathology, segmental disorders (اضطراب منصفي) and to mediastinal masses (كتلة منصفية). The mediastinum is a primary anatomic site for such diseases and is situated beneath the chest (hrudník), which itself is the overarching region for pleural conditions including pleurisy, pneumothorax and pulmonary collapse. Pleural disorders constitute the broader category for all pathologies of the pleural cavity (pleurální dutina); specific entities include neoplasms of uncertain behavior of the pleura, hemothorax, empyema and mediastinal‑pleural fistulas (right, left, bilateral). title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1725
content: Inspection (procedure) is a diagnostic category that incorporates several specific methods for visual and functional assessment of internal organs. Among its techniques are mediastinoscopy and thoracoscopy performed with ventilation of the lung, which allow direct visualization and sampling of mediastinal and pleural structures while maintaining adequate respiratory support. Another method included under inspection is the combination of paracentesis and endoscopic examination of the ileum, enabling fluid analysis together with direct endoscopic evaluation of the distal small intestine. In addition, inspection procedures serve as the method of choice for other bowel diagnostic procedures, encompassing a range of endoscopic and minimally invasive approaches used to assess gastrointestinal pathology. Together, these methods provide clinicians with essential information for accurate diagnosis, treatment planning, and optimal patient care.
